input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller, with physical components including motor and gearbox in the first axis.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation employs Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies including RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for multi-level abstraction (requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as SysML (IBDs and state machines in v1.7), ACME, AADL, and MontiArc (including MontiArc Automaton) model components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavior is detailed using MathWorks Simulink for simulation, and general-purpose programming languages like C++ and Java for code-based implementation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system's architecture is defined by a UML-based Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) comprising components such as Processing (for data processing and aggregation), Asset (representing the physical entity), and Model (simulation model). Section IV.B details the concrete integration where these components interact through specific relationships to form the digital shadow structure, ensuring coordinated data flow and system-wide orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems utilizes Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for interoperability, MQTT protocol for data streaming, and interfaces with the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl control system to enable real-time data exchange and system coordination.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for structural modeling (e.g., Integrated Block Diagrams), MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines for behavioral modeling, and MathWorks Simulink for analytical simulations. Data sources include contextual data traces from physical systems via digital shadow, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes. Data management involves integrating heterogeneous models across multiple tools (SysML, Simulink, C++, Java) and addressing stakeholder perspectives through coordinated modeling methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data from physical robots is streamed via MQTT protocol, enabling live-data linking with live measurements from the robot's sensors.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT protocol is used for streaming time series sensor data from physical to virtual components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process involves four key steps: problem identification, asset and model analysis, use or creation of calculations or models, and data identification. This pipeline enables the dynamic exchange of friction models for enhancing robot motion accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction, using both white-box and black-box models to enhance the robot's motion accuracy. Visualization is provided through a dashboard.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses SysML during development, and Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are referenced for compliance with industry standards.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot model, controller, and axis components mentioned in the text. It correctly states the system is a 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes' and notes it is 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'. It also correctly identifies the first axis components as 'motor and gearbox', though the text provides slightly more detail specifying types of gears and encoders.

The document explicitly mentions services provided by the digital shadow implementation, such as 'visualization in a dashboard' and using the derived data to 'improve the robot control’s accuracy'. Therefore, the claim 'Not in Document' is incorrect.

The extraction is highly specific and accurately reflects the tools and methodologies described in Section II.A of the source text. It correctly lists the MBSE methodologies ('RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT'), the Architecture Description Languages ('SysML... ACME, AADL, and MontiArc'), and simulation tools ('MathWorks Simulink', 'C++ and Java'). However, it omits other enablers mentioned elsewhere in the text, such as Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT.

The extracted description states 'Not in Document'. The document focuses on constructing a single digital shadow for the first axis of a robot (e.g., 'FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow'). It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins. Therefore, the information is absent.

The reference to Section IV.B for concrete integration is also correct as it details the 'Concrete Digital Shadow Model'.

The extraction correctly identifies the integration standards and protocols used. The description accurately reflects the data exchange and interoperability mechanisms described.

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering

Therefore, the extraction correctly identifies that this characteristic is not present in the document. Evidence: 'a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.', 'an automated one-way data flow between the state of an existing physical object and a digital object'.

However, the characteristic definition requests details on topology, bandwidth, latency, and security, which are not present. The extraction is specific to what is available but limited.

The source document discusses visualization via a dashboard and the use of Asset Administration Shells, but does not provide details regarding hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on-premise), computational storage requirements, or scalability mechanisms. The extraction correctly identifies that this information is missing. Evidence: No relevant information regarding hosting platforms, storage requirements, or scalability is found in the text.

block_5_judge.reasoning: The document does not contain information about lifecycle phases, development stages, representation types at different phases, transition management between phases, or lifecycle governance approaches. The focus is on implementing a digital shadow rather than its lifecycle management.

The extraction accurately reflects the methodology described in the source. The text lists four steps: '1) Problem identification', '2) Asset and model analysis', '3) Use or creation of calculations or models', and '4) Data identification'. It also mentions a pipeline for dynamic model exchange: 'We present a pipeline that enables the dynamic exchange of friction models.' The extraction captures these key technical details.

The extraction is well-supported by the abstract. The text states: 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction, using both white-box and black-box models to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy.' It also confirms the visualization method: 'visualization in a dashboard.' The extraction correctly summarizes the insights and user interaction.

The extraction is grounded in the text. The source mentions: 'Model-Based Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML' and 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)'. The extraction accurately identifies these standards compliance elements.

It does not contain any information regarding data governance, ownership policies, privacy protection, compliance with regulations, consent management, or data sharing agreements. Thus, the extraction 'Not in Document' is correct.

The source documents describe the robot's physical setup and control, mentioning a 'safety cell' that restricts the working range, but they do not address the specific areas defined in the characteristic description, such as cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation, or fail-safe mechanisms for the digital system. Therefore, the extraction 'Not in Document' is justified.
